description: The regex-literal token pattern `(?:(?:\[(?:\\]|[^\]])+\])|(?:\\/|[^\]/]))*` nests a `+` inside an alternation repeated by `*` over overlapping character classes, causing quadratic backtracking on unterminated-bracket input (e.g. a long run of `[` with no closing `]`); since this is Ace's Perl token

The dir capture is constrained to script|css, but the base capture group (.+) is unbounded and can include "../" sequences and slashes since only the trailing hash+extension pattern is anchored; path.join with this attacker-controlled base is not re-validated against PUBLIC_DIR before fs.existsSync/

The route regex's `(.+)` capture group for `base` can include path separators and `..` sequences (regex `.` matches any non-newline char), and this value is joined into the filesystem path via `join("public", dir, ...)` with no check that the resolved path stays under `public/`; `existsSync`/`sendFi

base = req.params[1] comes from the greedy regex capture (.+) which permits path separators and '..' sequences, and is concatenated with an attacker-chosen extension directly into path.join(PUBLIC_DIR, dir, base+'.'+ext) before res.sendFile with no canonicalization/containment check against PUBLIC_D

repoName and branchName are interpolated unescaped into a RegExp pattern; in this anonymize-utils context these values originate from user-supplied GitHub repo/branch identifiers, and git ref syntax permits regex metacharacters like parentheses and '+' that could form a catastrophic-backtracking pat

The `path` parameter is joined directly into the S3 object Key without visible sanitization, so an attacker-controlled path containing `../` segments could let `join()` escape the intended `repoPath(repoId)` prefix and read/overwrite objects belonging to other repos in the same bucket.

The regex capture `base` (req.params[1]) matches `(.+)` which permits path separators and `../` sequences, and is joined into `filePath` via `join("public", dir, base+ext)` then passed to res.sendFile without any check that the resolved path stays within the public directory; existsSync only confirm

repo.readme() returns raw content from a user-supplied/anonymized repository (untrusted data) and is passed directly to res.send(), which defaults to Content-Type text/html for string bodies; if the README contains HTML/script markup it will be rendered unescaped in the browser, enabling stored XSS.

This is jQuery 3.3.1 (vulnerable version, per the banner and version string 'x=3.3.1'), and the shown extend()/deep-merge implementation matches the known CVE-2019-11358 pattern: it recursively copies own-enumerable keys from a source object into the target without excluding '__proto__' or 'construc

The extend() function copies all enumerable keys from fns into obj via obj[s]=fns[s] with no denylist for __proto__/constructor/prototype, matching the classic unsafe-merge gadget (this matches tinysort's known prototype-pollution advisory); exploitability depends on whether callers ever pass attack

User-controlled req.query.q is only length-truncated (no escaping/parameterization) and is directly string-concatenated into a raw SQL query executed via sequelize.query, allowing injection e.g. via a crafted 'q' value containing quote characters.

req.body.email and req.body.password are concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query; the preceding regex check is a bypassable blacklist and doesn't even halt execution (no return after res.send), so the tainted query still runs regardless, making this exploitable SQL inje

req.body.email is concatenated directly into a raw SQL string; the regex check on line 15 is trivially bypassable (blocks only ['-;] literally) and even when it matches, execution falls through to the query anyway since there's no return/else after res.status(451).send(). This is the well-known Juic

req.body.email is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string via template literal with no escaping/parameterization before being passed to sequelize.query, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g., login bypass via ' OR '1'='1); this is the known intentionally-vulnerable Juice Shop login pattern.

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query without parameterization or escaping, allowing classic authentication-bypass/UNION-based SQL injection (e.g. email = ' OR '1'='1).

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string via template literals; the preceding regex check on line 15 only sends a 451 response without a return/next(), so execution falls through to the vulnerable query regardless, and even if it did block, blacklist-style

User-controlled `criteria` from req.query.q is interpolated directly into a raw SQL string via template literal; the `.replace()` call on line 5 doesn't mutate the string in place and its result is discarded, so no sanitization actually occurs before the tainted value reaches the sequelize.query sin

jwt.verify() is called on user-controlled token without restricting allowed algorithms, so an attacker who knows the RSA public key can forge a token signed with HS256 using that key as the HMAC secret, causing decoded (attacker-controlled) data to be trusted and cached as an authenticated user iden

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query with no parameterization or escaping, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g. auth bypass via ' OR '1'='1).
User-controlled req.query.q (only length-limited, not escaped/parameterized) is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string executed via sequelize.query, allowing classic UNION-based SQL injection.

`key` comes directly from `req.body.key` (untrusted user input) and is concatenated unsanitized into a filesystem path passed to `fs.existsSync`/`fs.readFileSync`, allowing path traversal (e.g. `key=../../../etc/passwd%00` or similar) to read arbitrary `.info.yml`-suffixed files outside the intended

`key` is used to index into a persistent, module-scoped `CodeFixes` cache object with no allowlist/sanitization shown, and appears to originate directly from a request parameter (per the `FixesRequestParams`/`VerdictRequestBody` interfaces below). If an attacker supplies key="__proto__", `CodeFixes[

req.body.id is passed unsanitized as the MongoDB query filter (and req.body.message as the update value), so an attacker can inject query operators (e.g. {"$ne": null}) to match and modify reviews they don't own; only authentication is checked, not ownership or input shape, making this the classic N

req.body.id is passed unsanitized directly into the MongoDB query as _id with no ownership check binding it to the authenticated user, so any authenticated user can forge updates to reviews they don't own, and unvalidated query-key input opens the door to NoSQL query-structure injection (e.g., opera

req.body.id flows unsanitized into the MongoDB filter as _id and req.body.message flows into $set without validation, so a caller can pass a NoSQL operator object (e.g. {"$ne": null}) instead of a plain id to match/update documents outside the intended scope; there's also no ownership check tying th

The code blacklists only the $ne operator on req.body.id before using it directly as a MongoDB query filter, but other NoSQL operators (e.g. $gt, $regex, $exists) remain unfiltered, allowing an attacker to pass an object instead of a scalar id and manipulate the query logic — a classic incomplete-de

WalletModel.findOne is queried using req.body.UserId directly with no check that it matches the authenticated session user, letting any caller retrieve another user's wallet balance (an IDOR/broken-access-control defect); the CWE-943 injection label is likely a mis-classification since Sequelize's d

The `email` value is passed unsanitized/untyped directly into a Sequelize `where` clause; without visible validation that it's a scalar string, an attacker who controls the JSON body can pass an object with operator keys to alter query semantics (classic NoSQL/ORM injection sink). Source validation

The filter only blocks forward slashes, so a single '..' still passes and escapes one directory level, and on Windows-hosted deployments Node's path.resolve treats backslashes as separators, letting an attacker use '..\..\..\' sequences to fully traverse outside logs/ since there is no backslash che

The check only rejects filenames containing '/', but path.resolve also treats backslashes as separators on Windows and a single '..' segment (which contains no slash) still climbs directories, so an attacker-controlled params.file can escape the encryptionkeys/ directory and reach arbitrary files.

req.body.captchaId is passed unvalidated directly into the Sequelize `where` clause; if it's an object (e.g. {"$ne": -1}) rather than a scalar, it can be interpreted as a query operator, letting an attacker match an arbitrary captcha record and bypass the intended captchaId binding since they alread

toUrl is user-controlled via query.to and passed to res.redirect after only an allowlist check (isRedirectAllowed) whose prefix-based matching (isUnintendedRedirect using startsWith) is known to be bypassable, and the code even has a built-in challenge that fires when the redirect target isn't one o

The blacklist only rejects forward slashes, so backslash-based traversal sequences (e.g. '..\..\..\etc\passwd') bypass the check entirely on Windows where path.resolve treats backslash as a separator, and the check doesn't normalize/validate the resolved path stays within 'ftp/quarantine/'. This is

req.body.id is passed unsanitized directly into a MongoDB query filter, allowing NoSQL operator injection (e.g. {$ne: null} or {$gt: ''}) to match/update arbitrary reviews; this is the known OWASP Juice Shop noSqlReviewsChallenge/forgedReviewChallenge intentional vulnerability.

The `id` value comes directly from `req.body.id` with no type validation or sanitization and is passed straight into `findOne({ _id: id })` (and later `update`), so a JSON body where `id` is an object (e.g. `{"$ne": null}` or `{"$gt": ""}`) lets an attacker inject MongoDB query operators, a classic

The `id` value used directly as the MongoDB `_id` filter at line 36 (and line 25) originates from the request body/params with no type validation or sanitization shown, allowing an attacker to pass an object like `{"$ne": null}` to manipulate the query — this is the known NoSQL injection pattern int

This is OWASP Juice Shop's deliberately vulnerable likeProductReviews route, where the `id` used in `_id: id` for both the update and findOne queries is taken directly from unsanitized request body/params (req.body.id in the actual source) without validation, allowing NoSQL operator injection (e.g.

This is the OWASP Juice Shop likeProductReviews route where the review `id` from user-controlled input flows unsanitized into MongoDB findOne/update queries, enabling NoSQL query injection (e.g. via operator objects like $ne/$gt) since the id is not shown being validated or cast to a safe type befor

The authorization check relies on `req.body.UserId`, an attacker-controlled value, being combined into the Sequelize `where` clause rather than deriving the user id from an authenticated session; a client can supply an arbitrary UserId (or, if Sequelize's legacy string operator aliases are enabled,

criteria is taken directly from req.query.q and concatenated into a raw SQL string via template literal in models.sequelize.query, with only a denylist regex (quotes, ';', 'and'/'or', '#') as a defense rather than parameterized queries/bind params — denylist filters are commonly bypassable (encoding

The code manually escapes only `<` and `>` via replaceAll, but omits `&`, `"`, and `'`, which is an incomplete/bypassable HTML-escaping approach compared to a proper sanitizer — exactly the weak-sanitization pattern this rule targets. Note this is inside Juice Shop's `codefixes` directory, which hol

An RSA private key is hardcoded directly as a string literal in source code and used for JWT signing, matching the rule's detection intent exactly; regardless of application context, embedding a private key in source is a genuine secret-management defect.

The HMAC key is a hardcoded string literal directly in source code used for cryptographic signing (crypto.createHmac), which is a genuine hardcoded-secret defect regardless of this being intentionally-vulnerable demo code (OWASP Juice Shop); anyone with the source can forge HMACs.

The HMAC key used to sign the deluxeToken is a module-level constant (privateKey) rather than being loaded from an environment variable or secrets store, and this token is used for security-relevant role verification elsewhere in the file, making a hardcoded key a genuine CWE-798 defect assuming pri

res.cookie('token', token) is called with no options object, so it uses Express defaults which omit HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite, leaving the auth token cookie exposed to client-side script access and cross-site transmission.

User-controlled `body.orderLinesData` flows directly into `vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', ...)` with no validation or sanitization, and Node's `vm` module is well known to be escapable, making this a genuine eval-injection/RCE sink reachable from untrusted request input (this is Juice S

The `fields` query parameter is attacker-controlled and used directly as a property key to read arbitrary properties off `user.data` and copy them into the response, bypassing the intended allowlist (id/email/lastLoginIp/profileImage) enforced in the else branch — e.g. a request with `?fields=passwo

The `email` value passed into the MongoDB query is derived from request data without any type/sanitization check shown in this snippet, so if it originates from req.body as in Juice Shop's actual implementation, an attacker can submit an object (e.g. {$ne: null}) instead of a string to inject MongoD

The allowlist only checks the file extension/name suffix (endsWithAllowlistedFileType) and strips a poison null byte, but never canonicalizes or restricts the resolved path to the 'ftp/' directory, so a filename containing '../' sequences (with an allowed suffix) passed to path.resolve('ftp/', file)

The blacklist only rejects filenames containing '/', but a value like '..' contains no slash and still causes path.resolve('logs/', '..') to escape the logs directory to its parent, and there is no canonicalization/containment check against the intended base directory afterward, allowing arbitrary f

The handler only blocks a literal '/' character before passing user-controlled `file` into path.resolve and res.sendFile, but this does not canonicalize or restrict the resolved path to the quarantine directory — traversal via backslashes (Windows), encoded separators, or other non-'/' techniques ca

User-controlled req.params.id is concatenated directly into a MongoDB $where clause, which evaluates arbitrary JavaScript server-side; when the noSqlCommandChallenge path is active the value is only truncated (not type-coerced), so injection payloads reach the query unsanitized — this is Juice Shop'

When the reflectedXssChallenge flag is enabled, `id` is only length-truncated (utils.trunc), not character-sanitized, and is interpolated directly into a `$where` JavaScript string executed by MongoDB, allowing NoSQL/JS injection via req.params.id; this is Juice Shop's intentional noSqlOrdersChallen

res.cookie('token', updatedToken) sets an authentication token cookie with no options object, so it lacks HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite attributes, making it readable via JS (XSS) and eligible for cross-site submission (CSRF); no sanitization or later cookie config is shown to mitigate this.

username is user-controlled (set via registration/profile) and a substring of it is passed directly to eval() when a regex pattern matches, allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution from attacker-supplied data; this is OWASP Juice Shop's intentional SSTI/code-injection challenge.

User-controlled username is substring-extracted and passed directly to eval() with no sanitization, only gated by a regex that fails open (falls through to eval whenever it matches), making it a genuine code injection sink reachable from untrusted profile input.

The code encodes other template values (e.g., `entities.encode(config.get('application.name'))` at line 61) before insertion, but `subs` is concatenated raw into a `<script>` tag with no escaping, and its origin is not visible/controlled at this point in the code — matching the known OWASP Juice Sho

The code explicitly mounts `serveIndex('ftp', { icons: true })` on the public `/ftp` route, enabling directory listing/browsing of the ftp folder's contents to any client; this is confirmed by the surrounding comments identifying it as the intentional 'directoryListingChallenge' in Juice Shop, so th

The code explicitly mounts serveIndex on '/.well-known' with directory listing enabled ({icons: true, view: 'details'}), which will render a browsable directory index of that folder's contents to any client; this is a direct, unconditional instance of the directory-listing pattern the rule targets,

serveIndex is mounted directly on '/encryptionkeys' with no auth/access-control middleware in front of it (unlike the '/support/logs' route below which has verify.accessControlChallenges()), so directory listing is genuinely enabled and exposed; this is intentionally-vulnerable OWASP Juice Shop chal

The code explicitly mounts serveIndex('logs', {view:'details'}) on /support/logs, enabling directory listing that exposes filenames/details of files in the logs directory to any client requesting that path; no code shown restricts access before the listing is rendered.

The username is extracted from the JWT presented in the Authorization header and logged via string concatenation without any newline/control-character sanitization, allowing an attacker who controls their own username (or forges a token claim) to inject CRLF sequences and forge log entries before th

e.printStackTrace() is leftover debug code in production payment-processing logic that writes exception details (including stack traces) to stderr instead of using the application's logging framework; this matches the rule's intent even though the exception is still rethrown afterward.

e.printStackTrace() is leftover debug code left in a production catch block instead of proper logging, matching the rule's definition; it writes internal stack trace details (including exception messages that may contain sensitive payment-processing info) to stdout/stderr in a payment integration mo

The code (based on the commented-out alternatives) instantiates AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding, uses a static IV constant (IV_P) rather than a random per-message IV, and provides no MAC/integrity check, which is the classic CBC padding-oracle and IV-reuse weakness; this is production encryption logic (Encrypt

The code explicitly instantiates Cipher.getInstance with a CBC/PKCS5Padding spec (confirmed by the commented-out NoPadding line above) and uses a static IV, making it vulnerable to padding oracle attacks and lacking integrity protection; this is genuine decryption logic, not test/mock code.

The code contains an actual e.printStackTrace() call left in a production catch block, matching the rule's exact target pattern of leftover debug code; it can leak stack trace details (internal paths, class names) to stderr/logs instead of proper logging. Impact is modest since output isn't directly

CSRF protection is explicitly disabled via .csrf().disable() for the /shop/** matcher, which includes authenticated, state-changing endpoints under /shop/customer/** requiring ROLE_AUTH_CUSTOMER. This exposes authenticated users to cross-site request forgery on customer actions unless CSRF is otherw

CSRF protection is explicitly disabled for /services/** while the same filter chain enables formLogin, which authenticates via session cookies; state-changing requests under /services/private/** (protected only by session/basic auth) can thus be forged from a malicious page since no CSRF token is re

This filter chain protects /auth/** using httpBasic() authentication, whose credentials browsers automatically cache and resend on subsequent requests (similar to cookies), making CSRF a real risk; disabling CSRF here removes protection against forged cross-origin requests to authenticated endpoints

e.printStackTrace() in a catch block is exactly the debug-code pattern the rule targets — it swallows a ServiceException while dumping the stack trace to stderr instead of proper logging, which is a genuine leftover debug artifact even though the trace isn't directly reflected to an HTTP client here

e.printStackTrace() is used as production error handling in a service facade instead of a logger, exposing internal stack trace details to stderr/console and matching the exact debug-code pattern the rule targets; it's not test/mock code and the method swallows the exception while leaking diagnostic

The catch block calls e.printStackTrace() directly, which is exactly the debug-code pattern the rule targets; this is production request-handling code (not test/mock), so the leftover debug statement is genuinely present and reachable, though its security impact is limited to log/stderr exposure rat

The code explicitly calls KeyGenerator.getInstance("DES") to generate a symmetric key used elsewhere for encryption/decryption (note the CIPHER constant nearby), and DES's 56-bit effective key space is broken by brute force with modern hardware, making this a genuine weak-cryptography defect regardl

The cipher is initialized with only a key and no IV (aes.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, key)), consistent with ECB mode (Java's AES default when no mode is specified is ECB), and the code encrypts data reachable via tokenizeString for use elsewhere in the app.

destinationFile is built by resolving the unsanitized multipartFile.getOriginalFilename() against destinationDir with no normalization/containment check, so a filename like ../../../etc/passwd lets Files.copy write outside the intended per-user directory.

This is WebGoat's intentionally-vulnerable JKU lesson: the attacker-controlled JWT's 'jku' header claim is used unchecked to build the JwkProviderBuilder URL and fetch the verification key, letting an attacker host their own JWKS and forge a validly-signed token; no allowlist/host validation is pres

The code explicitly uses MD5 (a cryptographically broken hash algorithm) via MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5") to hash a secret value, matching CWE-328; note this is WebGoat, an intentionally vulnerable training app where this weak-hash usage is the deliberate lesson content rather than an accidental

java.util.Random is not cryptographically secure and is used to scramble a password reset token; for the admin user it is seeded with key.length(), a tiny guessable value, making the reset link predictable/brute-forceable.

Both username_login and password_login are untrusted request parameters concatenated directly into the SQL string; despite using a PreparedStatement object, no parameter placeholders (?) are used, so the query is built via string concatenation and is directly injectable.

The SQL query string is built via direct concatenation of untrusted user input (username_login, password_login) into the query text before being passed to prepareStatement, so no parameterization/binding occurs despite using the PreparedStatement API — this is classic SQL injection, and it's the int

The `username` request parameter flows unsanitized into a string-concatenated SQL query executed via `Statement.executeQuery`, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g. `username_reg=' OR '1'='1`); note the very next block correctly uses a PreparedStatement, confirming the reported line is the actual uns

The password variable is initialized to the hardcoded literal "dave" and used as a fallback/default credential value compared against user input for authentication, which is a textbook hardcoded-password pattern regardless of the subsequent DB lookup attempt.

User-controlled `action` is concatenated directly into a SQL string and executed via Statement.executeQuery() with no sanitization or parameterization, allowing classic SQL injection; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable SQL injection lesson.

User-supplied `query` parameter from the HTTP request is passed directly into `statement.executeUpdate(query)` with no sanitization or parameterization, allowing arbitrary SQL execution; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable SQL injection lesson demonstrating exactly this flaw.
User-controlled `name` and `auth_tan` are concatenated directly into a SQL string with no sanitization or parameterization and executed via Statement.executeQuery, a classic SQL injection sink.

The query is built via string concatenation of an unsanitized 'action' parameter and executed with a plain Statement instead of a PreparedStatement; the only mitigation is replacing single quotes with double quotes, which is easily bypassed (e.g. via backslash-escaping or DB-specific quoting) and do

The query string is built via direct concatenation of user-controlled auth_tan (and other fields) into SQL text, then executed with statement.execute() without parameterization or sanitization, which is a textbook SQL injection pattern; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson code demonstr

The `accountName` (userid) parameter is concatenated directly into the SQL string and passed to prepareStatement, so the ? placeholder only covers login_count while userid remains unparameterized and attacker-controlled, allowing classic SQL injection.

The `query` parameter comes directly from user-controlled @RequestParam input and is passed unmodified into Statement.executeQuery(query), with no parameterization or sanitization, making it a direct, exploitable SQL injection sink.

The `query` parameter comes directly from an HTTP request and is passed unmodified into `Statement.executeUpdate()` with no parameterization, escaping, or validation, allowing arbitrary SQL injection.

The `column` request parameter is concatenated directly into the SQL query string used to build the ORDER BY clause, with no whitelist/validation shown before it reaches `prepareStatement`; PreparedStatement placeholders can't parametrize identifiers like column names, so this concatenation is explo

The zip entry name e.getName() is used directly to construct the extraction path via `new File(tmpZipDirectory.toFile(), e.getName())` with no validation against '../' sequences, then written via Files.copy — a classic Zip Slip that lets a malicious zip write files outside tmpZipDirectory.

setDebuggingStatus performs a state change (labelDebugger.setEnabled) but is mapped with @RequestMapping lacking a method restriction, so it is reachable via GET/HEAD without CSRF protection, matching the rule's exact concern.

A static PINCODE value intended to act as a secret/challenge token is generated with java.util.Random(), which is not cryptographically secure and is predictable/seedable, matching the weak-random CWE-330 pattern exactly as described by the rule.

java.util.Random() is used to select an index into a secrets/password array whose value becomes the actual Basic Auth credential stored in the session, so a non-cryptographic PRNG is directly used to select an authentication secret, matching the rule's intent.

The code deserializes a Base64-decoded byte stream directly from the untrusted `token` request parameter via ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no HMAC/signature verification or allow-listing before deserialization, matching CWE-502; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson demonstrating e

fromString() deserializes an arbitrary Base64-decoded byte stream via ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no ObjectInputFilter, allow-list, or type check; if the string parameter originates from user input (as it does in this WebGoat deserialization lesson), this is a classic unauthenticated deseria

The Cookie object is constructed and only setPath/setSecure are called before response.addCookie(cookie); setHttpOnly(true) is never invoked, so the cookie is sent without the HttpOnly flag, making it readable by client-side scripts (XSS-facilitated session hijacking).

The static `id` field is seeded from `new Random().nextLong()` and is directly used to build authentication/session identifiers via `GENERATE_SESSION_ID`/`AUTHENTICATION_SUPPLIER`, which is exactly the weak-RNG-for-security-token pattern the rule flags; `java.util.Random` is predictable and unsuitab

The JWT signing secret is selected via `new Random()` (non-cryptographic PRNG) from a tiny 5-element wordlist, then used as JWTSecretKeyEndpoint.JWT_SECRET to sign tokens — this is a textbook weak-RNG-for-security-token defect (trivially brute-forceable secret space of 5), matching CWE-330 exactly e

The Cookie holding the signed JWT access_token is created and added to the response without ever calling setSecure(true) (or setHttpOnly), so the token can be transmitted over plain HTTP and is exposed to interception/theft; nothing in the shown code mitigates this.

The access_token cookie (both here and on the success path at line 120) is created without calling setSecure(true), so it can be transmitted over plaintext HTTP; this is a genuine missing-Secure-flag defect on a security-relevant cookie, even though the specific flagged instance carries an empty val

The cookie carries a signed JWT access token and is added via response.addCookie without ever calling cookie.setHttpOnly(true), so it is genuinely readable by client-side scripts, matching the missing-HttpOnly defect class exactly as shown.

The access_token cookie (a JWT auth token, set identically at line 120 and cleared here at line 125-126) is created via `new Cookie(...)` and added with `response.addCookie(cookie)` without ever calling `setHttpOnly(true)`, so the browser will allow client-side scripts to read this authentication co

The cookie carrying the signed JWT access_token is created and added to the response via addCookie() without ever calling setSecure(true), so it will be transmitted over plain HTTP, exposing the auth token to network interception.

The cookie is created and added via response.addCookie() without ever calling setSecure(true) or setHttpOnly(true), so it will be transmitted over plain HTTP; this holds for both the token-bearing cookie (line 121) and the cleared cookie (line 126).

The code creates the auth cookie via `new Cookie(...)` and adds it via `response.addCookie(cookie)` without ever setting the SameSite attribute (or using a cookie/header API that defaults it), and there is no CSRF token check on this state-setting GET endpoint that issues a session/JWT auth cookie.

The code sets an authentication cookie (access_token) via the plain javax.servlet Cookie API with no SameSite attribute configured anywhere in the shown code, and there is no visible CSRF token check on the corresponding endpoints, so cookie-based auth here is exploitable via cross-site requests.

The `jku` value comes directly from an attacker-controlled, unverified JWT header (decoded before signature verification) and is passed straight into `new URL(...)` to fetch JWKs with no allowlist or validation, letting an attacker point the server at an arbitrary host — this is precisely the intent

The endpoint takes a raw user-supplied 'url' request parameter and passes it directly into ModelAndView's redirect: prefix with no validation, allowlisting, or sanitization, allowing arbitrary open redirects; the code comment even confirms this is intentional and unvalidated.

The traversal guard only inspects the raw, still-URL-encoded query string for literal '..' or '/'; request.getParameter("id") returns the decoded value, so an attacker can encode a payload (e.g. %2e%2e%2f) to bypass the check and still get a traversal-controlled path passed into new File(catPictures

The cookie is added via response.addCookie(cookie) without calling setSecure(true), so it will be transmitted over plain HTTP; this is a WebGoat lesson file but the code itself genuinely lacks the secure flag, matching the rule's exact class of defect.

The code constructs a Cookie and adds it to the response without ever calling setHttpOnly(true), which matches the rule's pattern exactly; however, since this specific cookie is used only to clear the session (empty value, maxAge=0), the practical exploitability/impact is low, tempering confidence.

The Cookie object is constructed and added to the response without ever calling setHttpOnly(true), so client-side scripts can read this authentication cookie, matching the missing-HttpOnly defect class exactly.

The cookie is created and added to the response without ever setting a SameSite attribute (only setPath and setSecure are called), and it is used as an authentication token (COOKIE_NAME/EncDec.encode(username)), so if consumed cross-site it lacks the SameSite defense-in-depth against CSRF. The code genuinely calls sqle.printStackTrace() in a catch block that swallows the exception, exactly matching the debug-code-left-in pattern the rule targets; this is real, unsanitized debug output in a live code path, not a test/mock file.

The code calls e.printStackTrace() in a catch-all Exception handler with a 'do nothing' comment, matching the rule's exact target pattern of leftover debug code; while the stack trace itself isn't returned to the client (only the password field is), printStackTrace is a genuine anti-pattern that can

User-controlled parameters (account, operator, injection) are concatenated directly into a SQL string with no sanitization and executed via Statement.executeQuery, which is exactly the classic SQL injection pattern this WebGoat lesson is designed to teach.

errors is built from compiler diagnostic messages (d.getMessage(null)) which can echo back fragments of the student-submitted source code, concatenated with raw "
" and returned via .output() in a ResponseBody; since "
" is manually embedded, the output is clearly rendered as HTML client-side

Severity is modest since output goes to stderr (no

The code directly calls e.printStackTrace(), which writes stack traces to stderr and is a genuine instance of active debug code left in a request-handling path; it also leaks e.getMessage() to the HTTP response, compounding the information-exposure risk exactly as the rule describes.

field1 is untrusted user input concatenated unescaped into an HTML string (cart) that is returned via .output(), which WebGoat renders as HTML in the response — this is a genuine reflected-XSS sink pattern, even though it's intentionally vulnerable lesson code by design.

This is WebGoat's reflected-XSS lesson (CrossSiteScriptingLesson5a), and cart.toString() reflects user-controlled cart contents directly into the response output field with no encoding shown, matching the same unsanitized pattern used in the success paths (lines 79, 84) — this is the intentional vul
